steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question assumes P7 gave equal actual ratings to impressionist and color-field paintings, then asks what the model predicted. If P7's actual ratings were equal but the model's predicted ratings differ for the two styles, there is a mismatch between prediction and reality — that mismatch is what the data show.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — P7's ratings for impressionist and color-field paintings would differ from one another.: The graph shows different model-participant correlations for P7 in the two styles; under the prompt's assumption that P7's reported ratings were equal, that difference supports that the model's predicted ratings differed by style. ✓
  • Choice B — P7 would derive more aesthetic pleasure from impressionist paintings than from color-field paintings.: This claims a specific direction (impressionist > color-field). The graph supports a difference, but the question does not specify which is higher for P7 — this goes beyond what the data show. ✗
  • Choice C — P7's ratings for impressionist and color-field paintings would equal one another.: This says the model predicted equal scores, but the graph supports the opposite: the predicted ratings differed by style. ✗
  • Choice D — P7 would derive less aesthetic pleasure from impressionist paintings than from color-field paintings.: Like B, this asserts a specific directional difference (color-field > impressionist) that the graph does not confirm. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
